channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Love Transmuted into Archive
item.description: Netflix’s latest Istanbul melodrama adapts Orhan Pamuk’s 2008 novel into a multilayered exploration of class and gender tensions, memory curation, and tragic limerence.

item.title: Being Eugène Atget
item.description: The new exhibition of Eugène Atget’s photography demands what we are getting worse at giving: time, stillness, and the willingness to see.

item.title: Back to the Canadian Future
item.description: Two new music-minded Canadian comedies are fueled by nostalgia for the recent past.

item.title: A New Catholic Resurgence?
item.description: Two sincere believers and noted public intellectuals make the case for God in a secular world.

item.title: Fame! A Misunderstanding
item.description: Albert Camus has long been misunderstood, but a new translation of his complete notebooks offers a corrective.

item.title: Spectacles of Repression: On Netflix’s “Beef”
item.description: Amanda Walujono reviews Netflix’s new dramedy “Beef.”

item.title: Sitting Apart
item.description: Gal Beckerman’s new book offers a thoughtful catalog of social resistance, from Diogenes to Alexei Navalny.

item.title: What Ever Happened to Aunt Gladys?
item.description: On Zach Cregger’s ‘Weapons’ and the figure of the hag.

item.title: Archives of Desire
item.description: Two recent biographies of Octavia Butler, drawing on her archive at the Huntington Library, ‘challenge us to reflect upon what it means to study a writer’s archive.’

item.title: The Epic of the Fear of Death
item.description: Simon Armitage’s new verse translation of the ‘Epic of Gilgamesh’ is unhurried and resplendent, archaizing without being antiquated.

item.title: Red Specters and Right-Wing Fever Dreams
item.description: A. J. A. Woods’s new book explores how the Right’s obsession with Cultural Marxism emerged from the backlash against the 1960s.

item.title: High and Dry
item.description: The ‘godfather of climate fiction’ offers a novel about finding a home on a despoiled planet.

item.title: Natural Election
item.description: Kathryn Paige Harden’s new book offers a defense of a kind of Scientific Calvinism that converges with the eugenicist ideals of the contemporary Far Right.

item.title: The Power of Fit
item.description: Laura B. McGrath’s new book looks at how the largely invisible work of literary agents shapes American literature.
